claude_reasoning: The young man's wife, father-in-law, and mother-in-law are referenced only as people he sneaked away from ('התגנב לברוח מאשתו וחותנו וחמותו'); none of them appear, act, speak, or are depicted in any scene. They function purely as contextual background explaining why the man left home.

The story features only male characters — Reb Zisha, Reb Elimelech, the Maggid of Mezeritch, an unnamed craftsman, the king, and the king's son. No women appear or are referenced in any way. The quoted verse 'באהבתה תשגה מאד' uses a feminine form but refers allegorically to divine love, not to any female character in the narrative.

The story concerns the relationship between Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch and the Baal Shem Tov — their meeting, a kabbalistic test, and Dov Ber's decision to become the Besht's disciple. No women appear or are referenced in any capacity.

The only women in the story are the doctor Garde's unnamed mistresses ('נשים אהובות לו'), referenced collectively as background context for his dissolute lifestyle and then dismissed one by one as he repents — they neither speak, act, nor appear in any depicted scene as individuals. They function purely as moral backdrop to illustrate the protagonist's spiritual transformation.
The story features only male characters — Rabbi Shalom of Belz, the innkeeper, and the Rabbi of Strelisk — with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.

The story involves only male characters throughout — Rabbi Levi Yitzhak, Rabbi Uziel Meizels, Rabbi Elimelech of Lizhensk, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and a coachman — with no women mentioned, referenced, or implied in any role.
The story features only male characters throughout — rabbis, a cantor, a wealthy host, guards, and congregants — with no women appearing, referenced, or mentioned in any capacity.

The doctor's mistresses ('האהובות') are referenced as an anonymous group that he habitually travels with and then dismisses one by one on his way to Mezeritch — they serve purely as backdrop illustrating his moral state and subsequent repentance, with no individual action, speech, or depicted scene. No other women appear or are referenced.
The story involves only male characters — the Baal Shem Tov, Rabbi Moharan, a Jewish innkeeper, and a servant — with no women appearing or referenced in any way.
The story involves only male characters — R' Zelig, R' Meir, the Besht, two men, and the innkeeper — with no women mentioned, referenced, or implied in any way.
The story involves a merchant, a thief, the Besht, and R. Tzvi the scribe — no women appear or are referenced in any way, even incidentally.

The innkeeper's wife appears in a depicted scene — she is sitting with her husband and directly addressed ('ראה אשתי'), placing her beyond mere relational mention; however she neither speaks nor acts, making the boundary with mention-only genuinely close. The non-Jewish woman ('נשג"ז') with whom the innkeeper sinned is referenced only as the object of his transgression, not as a character.

Women appear only in the phrase 'אנשים ונשים' (men and women) as part of the crowd that joined the funeral procession — an anonymous collective backdrop with no individual action, speech, or presence. No woman acts, speaks, or is depicted as a character.

Women appear only as part of the collective phrase 'אנשים ונשים וטף' (men, women, and children) within the Besht's letter, functioning as anonymous backdrop to the predicted commotion. No individual woman acts, speaks, or is depicted as a character.

The tax collector's wife appears in a depicted scene at the deathbed: she acts (steps away from him) and speaks (says 'perhaps he is sleeping a bit and it will rest him'), then checks on him and discovers he is dead — she is present and agentive in the scene, not merely referenced. A second woman (R' Yaakov's intended bride, 'לישא אשה') is mention-only as a destination, but the wife's scene-level presence and action lift the story to minor-character. The story's core — the Besht's vision and warning — does not depend on her.

The female orphan (היתומה) is mentioned once as having been raised in the Besht's home as part of the match arrangement, but she never acts, speaks, or appears in any depicted scene. The story's action centers entirely on the male figures — the Besht, R' Zeev Kitzes, R' Leib Kreminer, and the nobleman — and the financial miracle surrounding the wedding.

Women appear only once as part of the anonymous collective phrase 'אנשים ונשים' (men and women) who followed the mokhiach to the funeral — a pure backdrop reference with no individual woman acting, speaking, or driving any part of the plot.

The only female reference is 'וגם אצל הר"ר ברוך מתה בתו בשנה ההוא' — Rabbi Barukh's daughter is mentioned as dying that year, cited as evidence of the dispute's destructive consequences. She does not act, speak, or appear in any depicted scene; she functions purely as a contextual detail illustrating harm.

Tzipporah is referenced only within the Besht's retrospective explanation of his mystical experience ('when Tzipporah gave him food to eat for the first time'), invoking a biblical event; she does not act, speak, or appear in any depicted scene of the story itself. Per the guidelines, biblical female figures count only if they appear in the story's action, which she does not.

Two women are referenced: 'אשת רעהו' (his neighbor's wife) appears only as the object of the man's sin with no presence or action of her own, and Bathsheba ('בת שבע') is cited twice within a biblical analogy used as a teaching device, not as a participant in the story's action. Neither woman acts, speaks, or appears in a depicted scene.

מ' טובלע (Madame Tovle, wife of R. Shmuel) is an active agent whose decisions visibly shape the plot: she receives the Maiar's secret warning, decides to send her son-in-law to flee with the valuables (enabling his escape and later rise to prominence), and then initiates the appeal to the Baal Shem Tov — the event that triggers the story's climax. A reasonable counter-argument for catalyst-character exists since the Besht's refusal is the narrative's final point, but Tovle's independent decisions drive two distinct plot turns rather than merely providing a passive predicament.

The widow of R' Aharon (אלמנה אשת ר' אהרן) appears in a depicted scene at the story's close: she speaks ('בוודאי אין בתיבה מעות'), is persuaded to search the box, and finds both the hidden coins and the wrapped hairs — providing the empirical confirmation of the visionary dream. She acts and speaks as a character, but the story's main thrust is the afterlife drama of R' Aharon's soul, the demonic impersonation, and R' Gershon's nullification of it; her role is to verify the vision's authenticity rather than to drive or motivate the central plot.

The new mother (היולדת) is the pivot of the entire story — the accusation of mamzerut, the public crisis, and the rabbi's supernatural intervention all orbit her predicament — but the rabbi is the active agent throughout (refusing, declaring, sending, threatening). She confesses and speaks when confronted, giving her a moment of voiced presence, but her role is passive response to the rabbi's orchestrated revelation rather than independent agency driving the plot.

The wife's predicament (suspected sotah/infidelity) is the entire reason the story exists and the Gaon's miraculous intervention is the active narrative core; she appears in the central scene (summoned, present for the Torah reading, struck by the curses) but remains entirely passive — she does not speak or decide anything — making the Gaon the sole active agent. The tension between catalyst and minor-character (since she physically appears and reacts) warrants medium confidence.

The daughter of the Rabbi of Mezeritch appears in a depicted scene at the Shabbat table (servants seat her nearby, she is singled out as 'בת הרה״ק'), and she performs a visible action — upon becoming niddah she flees the table ('וברחה מעל השלחן') — which triggers the Rabbi of Mezeritch's outburst and the estrangement between the two rabbis; her daughters-as-group mention ('בניו ובנותיו') also activates the collective flag. She is between mention-only and minor-character since her action is involuntary and she has no speech or independent agency, but she does act in a depicted scene and her departure is a plot-turning moment, placing her in minor-character.

Two women appear only as relational references: the innkeeper's first wife is mentioned when he asks whether to divorce her (the Besht says no) and again when she dies at age sixty; the second wife is noted solely as the one who bore him two sons. Neither woman acts, speaks, is described, or appears in any depicted scene.

R' Yechiel Michel's wife in hard labor (מקשה לילד) is the crisis that motivates the Besht's final prophetic instruction and the story's climactic miracle — without her predicament there is no resolution to report. The Besht's wife also acts as a minor character (she asks where R' Yechiel Michel is and sends for him), but the higher tier is set by the laboring wife's catalytic role; 'many women' (נשים הרבה) present at the birth constitute an anonymous collective.

The wife of Reb Shbtai is an active agent throughout: she makes and keeps a promise, independently discovers and sells the old garment's silver buttons, prepares the entire Shabbat meal, dances with her husband, recounts the events to him in speech, and then answers the Besht directly with a consequential choice (a son over wealth) that produces the birth of the Maggid of Kozhnitz — the story's telos. Her decisions and actions visibly drive both the central miracle and the narrative's outcome, though one could argue 'catalyst-character' given that the frame centers on Reb Shbtai's piety and the Besht's perception.

The bride (כלה) and the female in-law (מחותנת/זוגתו) appear in depicted scenes — they travel to Kozhnitz and are examined by the Tzadik — but neither speaks, acts, or decides; the story's core turns on the young man's jealousy and the Tzadik's supernatural revelation that these figures are demons, not on the women's own predicament or agency. A case for catalyst-character is possible since the bride's existence anchors the entire deception, but her role is as passive instrument rather than a suffering subject whose situation drives a male protagonist to intervene.

The Gaon of Vilna's mother appears as an active character: she is persuaded by the Gaon's students and goes to her son to invoke the commandment of honoring one's mother, forbidding him to leave the house — an action the narrator frames as catastrophic ('בזה היתה תלויה הגאולה האמיתית'). She acts and her intervention has real narrative consequences, but she is a manipulated instrument in a subordinate episode of a story whose main thrust is R' Yisrael Palitzkier's spiritual journey to Mezeritch; removing her episode would not dissolve the core plot.

The wealthy heiress is an active driver of the plot: she independently devises the scheme of leasing shops and routing her own merchandise through the avrech's name to protect her reputation, instructs the matchmaker and her uncle, goes to inspect the goods herself, and at the story's climax delivers the key speech that explains the Rabbi's supernatural interventions to her husband — resolving the story's meaning. Her decisions (not merely her situation) visibly shape the marriage plot. The deceased first wife ('מתה עליו אשתו') is a bare mention with no scene presence. Confidence is medium rather than high because the Rabbi is ultimately the orchestrating agent and the heiress was coerced by illness, making a 'catalyst' reading defensible.
The story features only male characters throughout — the rabbis Pheivish, Moshe Leib of Sassov, and Tzvi of Zidichov — with no women mentioned, referenced, or implied in any capacity.

The young widow appears in depicted scenes, speaks multiple times, and makes active decisions that visibly shape the plot — agreeing to leave with the Besht, speaking to her father-in-law, voluntarily coming to confess before her wedding, and accepting (and fulfilling) the unusual penance — making her more than a passive catalyst. However, a case for catalyst-character is defensible since the Besht remains the primary agent who travels, arranges, and prescribes, while she is the soul being rescued.

The inn's maidservant (שפחת הבית) appears in a depicted scene, actively refuses the innkeeper's servant's sexual advance, and cries out (ותתן את קולה ברעש) — her actions directly trigger the false accusation and beating of the rabbi that forms the story's central incident. She acts rather than merely being mentioned, but the story exists to illustrate the rabbi's wisdom, humility, and spiritual refinement, not her predicament; she exits the narrative immediately after her brief action.

The unnamed woman actively confronts the rabbi with a theologically sharp rebuttal that silences him, directly shaping the story's climax and moral; the rabbi himself declares she defeated him. Her speech is the pivotal act, not merely her situation.

The story features only male characters throughout — Rabbi Yaakov Shimshon, an unnamed elderly sage, Rabbi Baruch of Medzhibozh, and the Baal Shem Tov — with no women mentioned, referenced, or implied in any way.
The story involves only male characters (the rabbi of the community, R' Nachman of Horodenka, R' Ze'ev Kutzes) at the funeral of the Besht; no women appear or are referenced in any way.
The story features only male characters throughout — the Besht, his disciples, the nobleman (אדון), and the prophet Elijah — with no women appearing, referenced, or implied in any way.
The story involves only two male figures — R' Ze'ev of Zhitomir and his student/son-in-law R' Shneur — with no women mentioned, referenced, or implied in any way.
The story involves only male characters — the Besht, R. Yosef the teacher, the doctor, and the townsmen — with no women appearing or referenced in any way.

The passage is a Hasidic homily on Exodus 8 attributed to R. Menachem Nachum of Makarov, focused entirely on morning devotion and cleaving to God; no women are mentioned or referenced in any capacity.
The story focuses entirely on the Rebbe Zusha and his male disciples during a penitential session; no women are mentioned, referenced, or implied anywhere in the text.
The story involves only male characters — Rabbi Elijah of Sokolivka, the Baal Shem Tov, an anonymous Jewish traveler, and a robber — with no women mentioned or referenced in any capacity.
The story concerns two male rabbis (R' Israel and R' Chaykil) meeting at a male innkeeper's home; no women are referenced, mentioned, or implied anywhere in the narrative.

No women appear or are referenced in this story; the phrase 'עיר ואם בישראל' is a biblical idiom describing the town's future status, not a reference to an actual woman. All named figures are male (R' Falik HaKohen, Rabbi Shmeryl, the Besht).

מרת אדל (the Besht's daughter) is a genuinely active agent: she goes to the river of her own initiative, engages the wealthy man in conversation, decides to disclose her father's identity and situation, and returns to deliver the invitation — a chain of decisions and speech acts that directly causes the entire resolution of the story. The king's mother (אם המלך) is also mentioned with attributed agency (interceding with the king), though she appears only in a reported summary rather than a depicted scene. Catalyst-character is defensible since Adel is offstage during the central Seder/soul-elevation scene and the narrative ultimately orbits the Besht, but her actions go beyond mere situation-as-motivation into visible plot-shaping.
The story involves two male rabbis (R. Zusha of Anipoli and R. Elimelech) and an encounter with the author of 'Chesed l'Avraham' in the upper worlds — no women appear or are referenced in any way.
The story features only male characters — Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka, the Baal Shem Tov, the rabbi of Polonnoye, and a non-Jewish nobleman — with no women appearing or referenced in any way.
The story involves the Besht, young men, townspeople, a judge, and a bear — no women appear, are referenced, or are implied in any role.
The story depicts a deathbed scene between a rebbe and his male disciples, with no women appearing, referenced, or mentioned in any way.

No women appear or are referenced in this story; all named figures are male (the Besht, Rabbi Gershon, Rabbi Shimshon, Rabbi Yosef). The kinship term 'גיסו' (his brother-in-law) denotes a male-to-male relationship and does not constitute a reference to a woman.

The two wives are mentioned only as being pregnant and giving birth offstage, functioning purely as a sign/proof within the letter — they do not appear, act, speak, or participate in any depicted scene. Their pregnancies serve as a miraculous token validating the Baal Shem Tov's letter, but they have no character presence beyond this reference.

The Baal Shem Tov's daughter appears as a present character in the story — she presses her father about Passover provisions, speaks to the arriving guest, and is included in the dangerous sea voyage — but the main narrative thrust concerns the Besht's journey and spiritual drama. A case for 'catalyst-character' is weak since she does not drive the central miraculous events, yet she is more than a mere mention, actively speaking and participating in depicted scenes.

The bride (כלה) who collapses as if dead is the central predicament of the first episode — the Besht travels specifically to save her, and her situation (including the dead wife's jealousy and the broken written promises) drives the narrative. However, the dead first wife is also a significant agent in the backstory (she extracted promises, then sought revenge), making this borderline between catalyst and major character; since the bride herself is largely passive during the rescue while the Besht acts, catalyst-character fits best.
The story involves only male characters — the Baal Shem Tov, his disciples, the elderly innkeeper, a bailiff, and a passing traveler — with no women referenced at any point.

Two unnamed women appear as characters in a depicted scene — they are bound in chains, speak to the protagonist explaining their plight, and their rescue drives a significant episode of the story. However, they are not the central catalyst of the overall narrative, which is about a man learning the quality of trust (bitachon); the women's episode is one illustrative incident within a larger frame story.

The innkeeper's wife appears in a depicted scene where she actively pressures her husband, curses him, and urges him to take on a partner — her nagging directly precipitates his pivotal decision to take God as his partner. However, she is not the main thrust of the story (which is about bitachon/trust in God), so she functions as a minor character whose actions move the plot forward without being its central focus.

An unnamed elderly woman appears in the depicted scene, crying out, warning the travelers of danger, and arguing with the Baal Shem Tov — she acts and speaks within the story. However, the main plot revolves around R' Yaakov's forgotten memories and the bishop's repentance, so she is a minor character whose removal would diminish texture but not the core plot.

The wife of the parnas (Parnass's wife) appears as an active character in a depicted scene — she wakes up, cries out, questions the intruder, and grabs his hat — which drives a key plot twist. However, she is not the story's protagonist or primary catalyst; the main narrative concerns R' David's exile and the exposure of the wicked preacher, so she functions as a significant but supporting character.

Tovele (the wife of R. Shmuel) appears as a named character who actively urges her husband to invite the Baal Shem Tov, and her initiative sets the story's events in motion; however, the main thrust of the narrative centers on the shochet's misconduct and the Besht's miraculous discernment, so she functions as a minor rather than catalyst character. The Duchess also appears briefly as an agent who sends guards, but neither woman drives the core plot.

The story involves only the Baal Shem Tov, his male disciples, and a nobleman (the 'Adon'), with no women referenced at any point. The entire narrative concerns the revelation of Elijah the Prophet disguised as a nobleman.
The story is entirely about male characters — the Maggid of Mezeritch, R. Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk, the Baal Shem Tov, and R. Yaakov Yosef — with no women referenced anywhere in the text.

The Rebbetzin initiates the conflict by going to buy fish and being publicly humiliated, and the Gvira (magnate's wife) is the antagonist whose actions (seizing the fish, insulting the Rebbetzin, refusing to appear, confessing under supernatural pressure) drive the entire plot forward — her decisions and speech actively shape every stage of the story. Both women act, speak, and make consequential choices, though the Rabbi is ultimately the agent of resolution; the boundary between catalyst and major is close, but the Gvira's active agency (instigating, defying, confessing) tips the balance toward major-character.

The duchess (Duchess) appears briefly as the one who desires the Jewish merchant and initiates the sin, but she functions mainly as contextual backstory/catalyst for the real story, which is entirely about the two male partners and the posthumous legal dispute. She is named only as background context for the transgression; the narrative's entire focus is on the male characters' deal, death, and rabbinical judgment.

The deceased former fiancée (Esther Sifra) appears as an active character in the story's central scene: she speaks, laughs, identifies herself, negotiates the terms of forgiveness, gives detailed instructions, and then vanishes supernaturally — her decisions and speech directly drive the plot's resolution. However, a case could be made for catalyst-character since the male protagonist is the one traveling and acting throughout most of the narrative.

The naked woman by the river is the pivotal figure whose desperate situation (near-frozen, unclothed after losing her horse and clothes) prompts the informer's decisive act of chesed that seals his repentance; without her predicament there is no final test of his transformation. However, a later anonymous collective reference ('anashim u-nashim ve-taf') also appears, justifying collective_women:true. The woman acts minimally (speaks, explains her plight) but the story's engine is her crisis enabling the male protagonist's merit, placing her closer to catalyst than major character, though the line is somewhat blurry.
The story is entirely about the Maggid Dov Ber's journey to meet the Baal Shem Tov and their encounter; no women appear or are referenced in any way.

The text is the famous letter of the Baal Shem Tov describing his soul-ascent, the Messiah's palace, and mystical teachings — no women appear or are referenced in any way. The brief metaphor of 'groom and bride' (חתן וכלה) is a quoted simile for divine joy, not a character reference.
The story involves only male characters — R' Aharon of Karlin, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and their male disciples — with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.

The orphan girl (יתומה) is referenced only as the subject of a betrothal arrangement and never speaks, acts, or appears in any depicted scene. The story's action centers entirely on the male rabbis present at the betrothal ceremony and R. Menachem Mendel's fainting episode.

Women appear only in the bishop's threat to destroy all the Jews 'from women to children' (מנשים ועד טף), a formulaic expression with no individual female character acting or appearing in a scene. No woman plays any role in the story's plot.

The baker's daughter who becomes R. Chaim's wife is referenced only as a passive object of the match ('a virgin daughter') and as fulfilling her role ('a kosher woman who does her husband's will'), with no speaking, acting, or depicted scenes of her own. The collective 'men and women and children' who escort R. Chaim out of Kraków are a backdrop reference.

Women appear collectively in the funeral crowd ('men, women, and children') and two married women appear as participants in the adultery scene that is the cause of the plague — they are named as such and their presence is integral to the plot revelation, but they do not act, speak, or make decisions; the story's active agents are the rabbi and the dead man. The agunah (abandoned wife) is also mentioned as a character whose case is resolved, giving her a slightly more individuated role, though she remains passive throughout.

Two women appear: the wife of R. Eliezer who escapes captivity and becomes a midwife (briefly described but functions as context/backdrop for the main narrative about R. Eliezer), and the non-Jewish wife given to R. Eliezer who eventually sends him home — the latter does act and speak (asks why he hasn't touched her, swears an oath, sends him away with money), which gives her some character presence. However, her role is minimal and instrumental to R. Eliezer's story rather than a developed character, making minor-character defensible but the lower tier is more appropriate given how briefly she appears.

Two women appear as characters with speech and actions: R' Adam's wife requests relief from poverty and then returns the taken goods, and the sister of R' Gershon speaks decisively to uphold their father's betrothal arrangement. Both act and speak in depicted scenes, but neither drives the main narrative, which centers on R' Adam and the Baal Shem Tov.

The Baal Shem Tov's wife appears briefly in a depicted scene where she is greeted by her brother and describes their hardships ('we were traveling from city to city and many troubles surrounded us'), giving her a small active presence. However, she is peripheral to the main stories (the frog/soul narrative and the wagon incident), so 'minor-character' fits better than 'mention-only', though she barely crosses the threshold.

The Baal Shem Tov's wife appears in a depicted scene — she speaks to the guest, explains her husband's pious customs (the twelve challahs, the mikveh), and her actions/words reveal his hidden identity — giving her a small but genuine character presence. However, the story's core plot does not depend on her; she provides texture and indirect characterization rather than driving the narrative.
The passage describes the Baal Shem Tov's solitary retreats in forests, caves, and mountains before his revelation, with no women mentioned or referenced in any capacity.

The story revolves around two afflicted young women (the nobleman's daughter in Pistin and the wealthy man's daughter in Elyitch), whose madness is the entire reason the Baal Shem Tov travels and performs his miracles. However, both daughters are purely passive — they are acted upon, whispered to, or prayed over — while the Besht is the active agent driving all plot events.
The story involves only male characters — the Baal Shem Tov and Rabbi Zeev the Av Beit Din — with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.
The story concerns male figures throughout — the Baal Shem Tov, the narrator's father-in-law (a shochet/scribe), and various male characters. No women are mentioned or referenced in any way.

The possessed/mad woman (אשה משוגעת) appears as an active character in a depicted scene — she speaks, reveals secrets, pleads with the Besht, and her exorcism is the central episode of that portion of the narrative. However, the story as a whole is a biographical account of the Besht's early life, and she is one episode among several; the wife (חמי's wife) is also briefly mentioned as refusing to divorce, indicating awareness of her husband's hidden greatness, which is a small but real action. The mad woman's role is substantial enough to exceed mere mention but the story's main thrust is the Besht's self-revelation rather than her predicament, placing her between minor-character and catalyst-character — the lower tier applies.

The foreign woman (wife of the mill owner) appears at the end, arrives weeping, and speaks to the tax-farmer in Russian — she acts and speaks in a depicted scene. However, her role is brief and peripheral to the main plot of exorcising the harmful spirit; she seems to report a minor incident rather than drive the central narrative.

The Baal Shem Tov's daughter appears as a present character in the story — she presses her father about food for Passover, speaks with him, and receives the guest — but she is not the central driver of the plot. The main narrative concerns the Besht's journey, the miracle of the childless couple, and the sea adventure; the daughter provides texture and context but is not the story's core agent or catalyst.

Adel (the Besht's daughter) actively drives the plot: she goes to the river, weeps, engages the wealthy patron, and runs back to tell her father — without her initiative there is no rescue, no Seder, and no miraculous intervention. However, the Besht remains the central miraculous agent, so 'catalyst' is also defensible; her active decisions tip the balance toward major-character.
The story features only male characters throughout — rabbis, the Baal Shem Tov, a doctor, and sons — with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.
The story features only male characters (the Baal Shem Tov, R' Gershon, R' Moshe) with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.

The wife speaks, advises, and actively initiates the plot by urging her reluctant scholar-husband to go to the Baal Shem Tov — without her speech and decision the journey never happens. However, once she sets the plot in motion she disappears, making 'catalyst-character' also defensible; her active, named speech that redirects her husband's inaction tips the balance toward major-character.

The story centers on male characters (the Ari, the groom/scholar, and a soul undergoing reincarnation). The 'mevashlos' (cooks) are briefly mentioned as a group performing an action, but they function purely as functional backdrop with no individual presence; the soul being reincarnated is grammatically treated as genderless/neuter in context. A case could be made for mention-only due to the cooks, but they are so peripheral that no-women is the most defensible primary tier.

The wife appears in several depicted scenes: she is told to unload the flour, she informs her husband when the flour gets wet, she is dressed in holiday clothes with the money, and the husband is told to buy her clothing. She acts and is addressed directly in the narrative, making her more than a mere mention, but she is not a driver of the plot — the story centers on the male protagonist's miraculous provision for Passover.
The story involves only male characters: the Baal Shem Tov, R' Gershon, and a non-Jewish man. No women are mentioned or referenced in any way.

The Baal Shem Tov's wife appears as an active character in the depicted scene — she answers the guest's questions, offers to cook the chicken, checks whether the knife is acceptable, goes to inform her husband, and later wakes the Besht to revive the fainted R' David. Her actions meaningfully advance the narrative, making her more than a mere mention, though the story's main thrust concerns the revelation of the Besht's holiness to R' David.
The story involves only male characters — a wealthy man, a learned tutor, the Baal Shem Tov, and his disciples — with no women mentioned or referenced in any way.

The story features multiple women with significant roles: a dying/unconscious bride (catalyst for the Besht's intervention), a deceased jealous wife (whose ghost/spirit actively attacks the bride and argues before the Besht's tribunal), and the bride who speaks at the end revealing the backstory. The deceased wife in particular acts as an agent (she tries to kill the bride, presents her case, and is rebuked), which pushes toward major-character, but the Besht remains the dominant active agent throughout, making catalyst-character the more defensible primary tier — though the boundary with major-character is genuinely ambiguous here.

The innkeeper's wife is referenced only as the mother of the newborn ('the infant born to him from his wife'), with no appearance, speech, or action in the depicted scenes. The story centers entirely on the Baal Shem Tov's miraculous intervention against the sorcerer.

The merchant's wife actively speaks, pressures her husband, and ultimately compels him to visit the Baal Shem Tov — her initiative drives the plot forward. However, once the husband departs, she becomes passive and the narrative centers on the male protagonist's interaction with the Besht, placing her closer to catalyst than full major-character.
The text contains no women characters, references, or mentions of any kind — it focuses entirely on the Baal Shem Tov's prayer, trembling, and interactions with male disciples and rabbis.

The widow (wife of the deceased) is the reason the Baal Shem Tov sends a message and she is the target of the action regarding the match, making her predicament central to the plot's resolution. However, she is largely passive/offstage, with the male figures (the deceased, R' Yosef, the Besht) as the active agents; a case for minor-character could be made if her role is seen as merely contextual.

The ambiguity between catalyst-character (her predicament/scheming drives the plot) and major-character (she speaks and acts) is real, but since the Gaon remains the active agent of the story's religious meaning and resolution, catalyst-character is the better fit.
